conditions.critical_condition_basis: Critical adsorption point (CAP) for EO unit where retention is independent of molar mass
conditions.evidence_text: The best results were obtained with the Luna HILIC column... as can be seen in Fig. 8, polyoxyethylenes with different number of hydroxy groups could be very well separated from each other... The question was now, whether these conditions really corresponded to the CAP for PEG. This is indeed the case, as can be seen in Fig. 10. The slight deviation from critical behavior in 97% acetone is no problem for samples with similar molar mass.
conditions.notes: Used for separation of PEG derivatives according to number of hydroxy end groups. Also used for PEG methacrylates and ethoxylated glycerides.
conditions.physical_address: Institute of Chemistry, Karl-Franzens-University, Heinrichstrasse 28, Central Polymer Laboratory/Molecular Characteristics (CePol/MC), A-8010 Graz, Austria

conditions.critical_condition_basis: Critical adsorption point (CAP) for EO unit
conditions.evidence_text: As has already been described previously [40], a CAP for PEG could be found on a diol phase in about 80 wt.% acetone–water. Under such conditions, polypropylene glycol (PPG) eluted in SEC mode. The same behavior is also observed on diol phases in 90% acetone (Fig. 4). We have thus analyzed polyoxyethylenes with different functionality on such a column and found, that they were really separated according to the number of hydroxy groups (Fig. 5).
conditions.notes: Text mentions 80% as a previously described CAP, but explicitly states the 90% condition was used for the analysis of polyoxyethylenes with different functionality in this study.
conditions.physical_address: Institute of Chemistry, Karl-Franzens-University, Heinrichstrasse 28, Central Polymer Laboratory/Molecular Characteristics (CePol/MC), A-8010 Graz, Austria

conditions.critical_condition_basis: Critical adsorption point (CAP) for EO unit

conditions.critical_condition_basis: Critical adsorption point (CAP) for EO unit
conditions.evidence_text: on the Discovery column, the same elution order was observed, but with much poorer efficiency (Fig. 7).
conditions.notes: Figure 7 caption specifies 'Discovery HS PEG, 95.9 wt-% acetone'. The text implies this is a critical condition setup for PEG to separate by end groups, though with poorer efficiency.

conditions.evidence_text: The best results were obtained with the Luna HILIC column (which is typically used for other purposes [41–44]): as can be seen in Fig. 8, polyoxyethylenes with different number of hydroxy groups could be very well separated from each other.
conditions.notes: Used for separation of PEG derivatives according to number of hydroxy end groups. Also used for PEG methacrylates and ethoxylated glycerides.

conditions.evidence_text: In the case of PEG derivatives, this means, that retention depends on these parameters, on the number of ethylene oxide (EO) units and the number of hydroxy groups. As is shown in Fig. 1, the individual oligomers of PEG derivatives can be resolved on a silica column in 90–95% acetone, and retention increases in the order polyethylene glycol dimethyl ether < polyethylene glycol monomethyl ether (PEG-MME) < PEG.
conditions.physical_address: Institute of Chemistry, Karl-Franzens-University, Heinrichstrasse 28, Central Polymer Laboratory/Molecular Characteristics (CePol/MC), A-8010 Graz, Austria

conditions.evidence_text: On a reversed phase column, this can even be achieved, if the end groups are not very different from the repeat unit, as is the case with PEG and PEG-MME: under critical conditions for PEG on a reversed phase column in acetonitrile–water, acetone–water or methanol–water mobile phases both series elute as narrow peaks, which are well separated from each other [4, 19, 20].
conditions.physical_address: Institute of Chemistry, Karl-Franzens-University, Heinrichstrasse 28, Central Polymer Laboratory/Molecular Characteristics (CePol/MC), A-8010 Graz, Austria
